items.title: [Beginner]: add regression tests for artifact integrity failure modes
items.description:
### 🐥 Beginner Friendly
Welcome! This is a **Beginner Issue** — a natural next step after Good First Issues.

It expects you to:
- Read existing code and follow its patterns
- Own a small implementation decision
- Write or extend tests

**Difficulty:**
- Basic Python (reading and writing functions) required
- No external API experience needed
- Familiarity with the contribution workflow expected

> [!IMPORTANT]
> We recommend completing at least one Good First Issue before attempting a Beginner Issue.
>
> **You must be assigned before starting** — comment `/assign` to claim this issue.
> PRs submitted without a prior assignment will be closed.

### 👾 Description of the Issue
`run_integrity_checks()` in `scripts/quality.py` is a key guardrail for publish safety, but several important failure paths are not explicitly regression-tested end-to-end.

Add targeted tests for scenarios such as:
- missing required artifacts (`docs/jobs.json`, `docs/health.json`)
- invalid JSON payloads
- jobs/health count mismatch detection
- contract validation error propagation in report payload

Focus on deterministic, temp-directory-based tests only (no live network calls).

### ✅ Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The change is implemented in the correct file/function
- [ ] Edge cases are handled (None, empty string, NaN, Unicode)
- [ ] All existing tests pass (`make test`)
- [ ] New tests are added covering the changed behavior
- [ ] PR is linked to this issue with `Fixes #<number>`
- [ ] PR title follows Conventional Commits format

### 🏗️ Which area does this touch?
🧪 Tests (tests/)

items.title: [Good First Issue]: add focused unit tests for publish helper
items.description:
### 🆕 Newcomer Friendly
This is a **Good First Issue** — a guided, well-scoped task designed for contributors
who are new to this repository.

#### What you'll do
- ✅ Read a small part of the codebase
- ✅ Make a focused, well-defined change
- ✅ Write or extend a test
- ✅ Submit and merge a pull request

#### Support
The maintainer actively monitors Good First Issues and will respond to questions
within 24–48 hours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This issue does not require prior scraping or API experience.**
>
> - Basic Python and Git are sufficient
> - You must be assigned before starting — comment `/assign` to claim this issue
> - Read [CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/ambicuity/New-Grad-Jobs/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md)
>   before opening a PR

> [!NOTE]
> ⏱️ **Typical time to complete:** 30–90 minutes (once local setup is done)
> 🧩 **Difficulty:** Small, well-contained change
> 🎓 **Best for:** First-time contributors

### 👾 Task Description
Add a small dedicated test module for `scripts/publish.py::write_json_artifact`.

Target behaviors to verify:
- creates missing parent directories automatically
- writes valid JSON payload with pretty indentation
- preserves UTF-8 content
- writes a trailing newline (`\n`) at EOF

Suggested location:
- new tests in `tests/test_publish.py`

### ✅ Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tests are added for `write_json_artifact` parent-directory creation behavior
- [ ] Tests assert output file content is valid JSON with expected structure
- [ ] Tests verify trailing newline is present at end of written artifact
- [ ] Existing tests still pass (`make test`)
- [ ] PR is linked to this issue with `Fixes #<number>`
- [ ] PR title follows Conventional Commits format

items.title: [Intermediate]: add deterministic test coverage for evaluate_jobs sidecar
items.description:
### 🧩 Intermediate Contributors
This issue is intended for contributors who already have experience with this repository
and the contribution workflow.

You should feel comfortable:
- Navigating `scripts/update_jobs.py`, `config.yml`, and `tests/`
- Understanding how `filter_jobs()`, `deduplicate_jobs()`, and `categorize_job()` work
- Following existing patterns without step-by-step guidance
- Writing non-trivial pytest tests with mocking

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **You must be assigned before starting** — comment `/assign` to claim this issue.
> PRs submitted without a prior assignment will be closed.

### 🐞 Problem Description
`scripts/evaluate_jobs.py` is part of the published analytics sidecar path, but it has little to no direct test coverage for:
- eligibility gating logic (`_is_eligible`)
- scoring heuristics (`_score_job`)
- output artifact shape and metadata consistency in `main()`

This creates risk that silent scoring/contract regressions ship unnoticed.

### 💡 Expected Solution
Add deterministic tests covering:
- `_is_eligible` include/exclude behavior for closed/malformed jobs
- `_score_job` score/confidence bounds and reason-code generation
- `main()` behavior with a temporary `docs/jobs.json` fixture, asserting generated artifact shape and key metadata fields

Keep tests network-free and deterministic. Do not change scoring policy in this issue.

### ✅ Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Problem is resolved per the description above
- [ ] Edge cases handled: None, NaN, empty string, Unicode, malformed data
- [ ] All existing `pytest tests/` pass
- [ ] New tests added for changed logic (deterministic — no live network calls)
- [ ] `pre-commit run --all-files` passes
- [ ] PR is linked to this issue with `Fixes #<number>`
- [ ] PR title follows Conventional Commits format

### 🏗️ Which area does this touch?
🧪 Tests (tests/)

### 🔗 Additional Context
- Target module: `scripts/evaluate_jobs.py`
- Related contract helpers: `scripts/contracts.py`
- Related integrity checks: `scripts/quality.py`

items.title: [Feature]: add backend/frontend location taxonomy contract checks to prevent filter drift
items.description:
### Problem
Location semantics are maintained in multiple places (`scripts/update_jobs.py` and `docs/app.js`). These can drift independently, leading to inconsistent filtering UX (backend accepts values that UI cannot target, or UI filters that do not map cleanly to backend acceptance).

### Proposed Solution
Add a contract check (test and/or generated contract artifact) that enforces location taxonomy alignment between backend and frontend:
- parity for supported country set (USA/Canada/India/Remote semantics)
- consistency checks for representative state/province token coverage
- CI failure on incompatible drift unless both sides are updated intentionally

### Alternatives Considered
- Rely on manual coordination between scraper and frontend changes (drift risk remains high).
- Keep documentation-only guidance without enforcement (not CI-protected).

### 🏗️ Which area does this affect?
🧪 Tests (tests/)

### Constraints Check
- [x] This does NOT require an external database (PostgreSQL, Redis, SQLite, etc.)
- [x] This does NOT require a frontend framework (React, Vue, Next.js, Tailwind)
- [x] This does NOT require an external orchestrator (Airflow, Celery, etc.)

items.title: [Feature]: add publish-time URL safety validation for jobs artifact output
items.description:
### Problem
The pipeline validates schema/contracts but does not enforce a strict publish-time URL safety gate before writing `docs/jobs.json`. Unsafe or malformed links (non-http schemes, localhost/private-network targets, invalid hosts) could be published into public artifacts.

### Proposed Solution
Add publish-time URL safety validation before artifact write/finalization:
- allow only `http`/`https`
- reject localhost/private IP ranges/metadata endpoints/malformed hosts
- surface blocked-count telemetry in health output
- optionally fail fast or mark degraded when violations exceed a threshold

### Alternatives Considered
- Rely on liveness checks only (too late: unsafe links may already be published).
- Manual cleanup through issue reports (reactive and inconsistent).

### 🏗️ Which area does this affect?
🤖 Core scraper (scripts/update_jobs.py)

### Constraints Check
- [x] This does NOT require an external database (PostgreSQL, Redis, SQLite, etc.)
- [x] This does NOT require a frontend framework (React, Vue, Next.js, Tailwind)
- [x] This does NOT require an external orchestrator (Airflow, Celery, etc.)

items.title: [Feature]: canonicalize job URLs before deduplication and job_id generation
items.description:
### Problem
Deduplication and `job_id` generation currently depend on raw URLs. Equivalent listings often differ only by tracking/query variants (for example `gh_jid`, UTM params, reordered query strings), which can produce duplicate jobs and unstable IDs for the same posting.

### Proposed Solution
Canonicalize URLs before passing them into dedup (`get_job_key`) and `compute_job_id` identity logic:
- normalize host/scheme casing
- remove known tracking params (for example UTM-style parameters)
- sort remaining query params deterministically
- trim safe trailing-slash noise

Use canonical URL for identity/dedup while preserving original apply URL as needed for display.

### Alternatives Considered
- Keep exact URL matching only (still allows duplicates from link variants).
- Use fuzzy title/company dedup only (higher false-positive risk than deterministic URL normalization).

### 🏗️ Which area does this affect?
🤖 Core scraper (scripts/update_jobs.py)

### Constraints Check
- [x] This does NOT require an external database (PostgreSQL, Redis, SQLite, etc.)
- [x] This does NOT require a frontend framework (React, Vue, Next.js, Tailwind)
- [x] This does NOT require an external orchestrator (Airflow, Celery, etc.)

items.description:

The watchdog detected that `docs/jobs.json` has not been updated in **93 minutes**.

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Last updated | `2026-03-18T01:05:29Z` |
| Stale for | 93 minutes |
| Update cycle | Every 5 minutes |

## What to check

1. **Actions tab**: Is the `Update New Grad Jobs` workflow failing? Check [Actions](https://github.com/ambicuity/New-Grad-Jobs/actions/workflows/update-jobs.yml)
2. **Branch protection**: Did a new ruleset get added that blocks direct push?
3. **Scraper errors**: Is the scraper producing 0 jobs (all sources failing)?

## Auto-close

Close this issue once the board is confirmed fresh again.

*Filed automatically by the Board Freshness Watchdog workflow.*

items.title: [Intermediate]: filter out non-engineering 'network' roles that leak into the board
items.description:
### 🧩 Intermediate Contributors
This issue is intended for contributors who already have experience with this repository
and the contribution workflow.

You should feel comfortable:
- Navigating `scripts/update_jobs.py`, `config.yml`, and `tests/`
- Understanding how `filter_jobs()`, `deduplicate_jobs()`, and `categorize_job()` work
- Following existing patterns without step-by-step guidance
- Writing non-trivial pytest tests with mocking

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **You must be assigned before starting** — comment `/assign` to claim this issue.
> PRs submitted without a prior assignment will be closed.

### 🐞 Problem Description
The project aims to surface entry-level software, infrastructure, and networking roles, but the current filter configuration is broad enough that some non-engineering "network" jobs leak into the dataset.

Examples already present in `docs/jobs.json` include titles like:

- `Provider Network Associate`
- `Associate, Network Contracting`

These are valid jobs, but they are not aligned with the repository's core mission of curating quality new grad software/infrastructure roles. They create clutter in the same track that should contain roles like `Network Engineer`, `Network Security Engineer`, and `Systems Engineer, Networks`.

This likely comes from the interaction between:

- broad `new_grad_signals` like `associate`
- broad `track_signals` like `network`
- permissive filtering in `filter_jobs()`

### 💡 Expected Solution
Tighten the filter logic so non-engineering healthcare/provider-contracting "network" titles are excluded without losing legitimate networking/infrastructure roles.

The solution should:

- preserve real engineering titles such as `Network Engineer` and `Network Security Engineer`
- avoid hard-coding company-specific exclusions if a title- or pattern-based rule is sufficient
- keep the overall job schema unchanged

### ✅ Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] False positives like `Provider Network Associate` no longer pass the filter
- [ ] Legitimate engineering/networking titles still pass the filter
- [ ] Edge cases handled: None, NaN, empty string, Unicode, malformed data
- [ ] All existing `pytest tests/` pass
- [ ] New tests added for changed logic (deterministic — no live network calls)
- [ ] `pre-commit run --all-files` passes
- [ ] PR is linked to this issue with `Fixes #<number>`
- [ ] PR title follows Conventional Commits format

### 🏗️ Which area does this touch?
🤖 Core scraper (scripts/update_jobs.py)

### 🔗 Additional Context
- Relevant functions: `filter_jobs()`, `has_new_grad_signal()`, `has_track_signal()`
- Evidence is visible in the generated dataset under `docs/jobs.json`

items.title: [Beginner]: expand infrastructure keyword coverage for network automation, NOC, and network performance roles
items.description:
### 🐥 Beginner Friendly
Welcome! This is a **Beginner Issue** — a natural next step after Good First Issues.

It expects you to:
- Read existing code and follow its patterns
- Own a small implementation decision
- Write or extend tests

**Difficulty:**
- Basic Python (reading and writing functions) required
- No external API experience needed
- Familiarity with the contribution workflow expected

> [!IMPORTANT]
> We recommend completing at least one Good First Issue before attempting a Beginner Issue.
>
> **You must be assigned before starting** — comment `/assign` to claim this issue.
> PRs submitted without a prior assignment will be closed.

> [!TIP]
> **You should be comfortable with:**
> - Forking, branching, committing, and opening a PR without a tutorial
> - Reading Python code you did not write and following its patterns
> - Running `make test` and fixing failures before pushing
> - Rebasing onto `main` to resolve merge conflicts

### 👾 Description of the Issue
The current `infrastructure_sre` keyword list in `scripts/update_jobs.py` includes `network engineer`, but it misses several networking titles that are common in entry-level infrastructure hiring and already show up in real boards:

- `network automation engineer`
- `network performance engineer`
- `noc engineer`
- `network operations engineer`

That creates a quality gap where networking roles can fall into `other`, even though the project README explicitly says the board tracks network-focused roles.

Update `CATEGORY_PATTERNS['infrastructure_sre']['keywords']` so these titles are classified consistently, and add tests that prove the behavior.

Look at:

- `scripts/update_jobs.py`
- `tests/test_categorize.py`
- live references in `docs/jobs.json` such as SpaceX networking roles

Do not create a brand-new top-level category for this task. The goal is better classification within the existing `Infrastructure & SRE` bucket.

**Before claiming:**
- Check the issue labels to see what area this touches
- Review the CodeRabbit AI plan for a rough implementation overview
- If this feels like too big a step, try a Good First Issue first

### ✅ Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Networking titles listed above classify to `infrastructure_sre`
- [ ] The change is implemented in `scripts/update_jobs.py`
- [ ] All existing tests pass (`make test`)
- [ ] New tests are added covering the changed behavior
- [ ] PR is linked to this issue with `Fixes #<number>`
- [ ] PR title follows Conventional Commits format

### 🏗️ Which area does this touch?
🤖 Core scraper (scripts/update_jobs.py)

items.title: [Intermediate]: Expand test_config.py to cover filter_jobs, categorize_job, and deduplicate_jobs logic
items.description:
### Contributor Level

This is an intermediate issue. You need to understand how to write Python tests against functions that have no external dependencies, and how to read existing functions carefully enough to design meaningful test cases.

Prerequisites:
- Comfortable writing standalone Python test functions
- Able to import and call functions from a separate module
- Understands the difference between testing behavior vs. testing implementation

Estimated time to complete: 4-6 hours, including reading the source functions and designing edge cases.

---

### Problem Description

`test_config.py` is the entire test suite for this repository. It does one thing: load `config.yml` and assert that 10,000 or more companies are present. It does not cover any of the core filtering, categorization, or deduplication logic that the scraper depends on.

This means a regression in `filter_jobs()`, `categorize_job()`, or `deduplicate_jobs()` can silently ship to production and corrupt the live job board. The CI workflow (`ci.yml`) runs `python test_config.py` but this provides no confidence in the correctness of the data pipeline.

---

### Proposed Solution

Create a new file `scripts/test_scraper_logic.py` that exercises the core stateless functions using synthetic input data. No network calls, no API keys, and no external dependencies are required.

The file should follow the same runner pattern as `test_config.py` — a plain Python script that exits with code 0 on success and 1 on failure.

Minimum required test coverage:

**`categorize_job(title, description)`**
- A title containing "Software Engineer" maps to `software_engineering`
- A title containing "Data Scientist" maps to `data_ml`
- A title with no matching keyword maps to `other`
- Category ID, name, and emoji fields are all present in the return value

**`is_valid_location(location)`**
- Returns `True` for: `"Remote"`, `"San Francisco, CA"`, `"New York, NY"`, `"Toronto, Canada"`, `"Bangalore, India"`
- Returns `False` for: `"London, UK"`, `"Berlin, Germany"`, `""`, `None` (check the function signature before passing `None`)

**`deduplicate_jobs(jobs)`**
- A list with two identical jobs (same company, title, URL) returns one entry
- A list with two jobs differing only by URL returns two entries
- An empty list returns an empty list

**`normalize_date_string(posted_at)`**
- `"Posted Today"` returns today's date in `YYYY-MM-DD` format
- `"Posted Yesterday"` returns yesterday's date
- `"Posted 3 Days Ago"` returns a date 3 days in the past
- A string that matches no pattern is returned unchanged

After writing your tests, update `.github/workflows/ci.yml` to also run `python scripts/test_scraper_logic.py` in the `Run unit tests` step.

---

### Background Research

Read these functions before writing any tests. Understanding their exact return types and edge case handling is essential for writing correct assertions:

- `categorize_job()` — line ~278
- `is_valid_location()` — line ~1067
- `deduplicate_jobs()` — line ~973, and its helper `get_job_key()` at line ~950
- `normalize_date_string()` — line ~1000

---

### Testing Your Changes

Run your test file locally before opening a PR:

```
cd /path/to/New-Grad-Jobs
python scripts/test_scraper_logic.py
```

All tests must pass with exit code 0. Do not use AI to generate your test assertions — derive them from reading the actual function logic.

---

### Acceptance Criteria

- `scripts/test_scraper_logic.py` exists and covers all four functions listed above
- At least 3 test cases per function, including at least one edge case each
- The file exits with code 0 on success and code 1 on any failure
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` runs the new test file in the `Run unit tests` step
- `python -m py_compile scripts/test_scraper_logic.py` passes

---

### Done Checklist

Before opening your PR, confirm:

- [ ] `python scripts/test_scraper_logic.py` passes locally with all tests green
- [ ] `ci.yml` is updated to run the new test file
- [ ] Test cases cover the four functions listed above with edge cases
- [ ] No AI-generated test assertions — each assertion was derived from reading the source function
- [ ] Commit message follows Conventional Commits format: `test(scraper): add logic tests for filter, categorize, and dedup functions`
- [ ] `README.md` has not been modified (it is auto-generated)

---

### Additional Context

- Files to work in: `scripts/test_scraper_logic.py` (new), `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (update)
- Functions under test: `scripts/update_jobs.py`
- Related guide: [CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/ambicuity/New-Grad-Jobs/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md)

items.title: [Intermediate]: Replace bare except clauses in date parsing functions with explicit exception types
items.description:
### Contributor Level

This is an intermediate issue. You need to understand how Python exception handling works and be comfortable reading a function end-to-end before making changes. You do not need to run the scraper against live APIs to complete this.

Prerequisites:
- Familiar with Python exception handling (`try/except`, exception hierarchies)
- Able to identify what exceptions a block of code can realistically raise
- Comfortable running `python -m py_compile` and basic local validation

Estimated time to complete: 2-4 hours, including research and testing.

---

### Problem Description

Three functions in `scripts/update_jobs.py` use bare `except:` clauses, which catch all exceptions including `SystemExit`, `KeyboardInterrupt`, and `MemoryError`. This pattern makes debugging nearly impossible when date parsing fails in production, because the error is swallowed silently and the job receives a fallback value with no diagnostic output.

Affected functions and lines:

- `format_posted_date()` — line ~1270: bare `except:` silently returns `"Unknown"`
- `get_iso_date()` — line ~1283: bare `except:` silently returns `""`
- `get_sort_date()` (inner function inside `generate_jobs_json()`) — line ~1308: bare `except:` returns `datetime.min`

Current pattern:

```python
def format_posted_date(posted_at: str) -> str:
    try:
        ...date parsing logic...
    except:
        return "Unknown"
```

When a new date format arrives from a job board API, this silently produces `"Unknown"` in the live job board with no log output and no way to diagnose the root cause.

---

### Proposed Solution

Replace all bare `except:` clauses in the three functions above with an explicit exception tuple covering the exceptions that date parsing can realistically raise. Add a diagnostic print statement so failures are visible in the GitHub Actions logs.

The correct exception types for date parsing in this codebase are: `ValueError`, `TypeError`, `OverflowError`, and `OSError` (the last covers timestamp-to-datetime conversion failures on some platforms).

Reference implementation:

```python
def format_posted_date(posted_at) -> str:
    try:
        ...date parsing logic...
    except (ValueError, TypeError, OverflowError, OSError) as e:
        print(f"  Warning: Could not parse date '{posted_at}': {e}")
        return "Unknown"
```

Apply the same change to `get_iso_date()` and `get_sort_date()`. The print prefix should match the existing style used elsewhere in the file (e.g., `"  Warning:"` or `"  Error:"`).

---

### Background Research

Before writing your fix, read the following to understand what exceptions are realistic:

- `dateutil.parser.parse()` raises `ValueError` and `OverflowError` on unparseable input
- `datetime.fromtimestamp()` raises `OSError` on out-of-range timestamps on some platforms
- `normalize_date_string()` (line ~1000) can be called with non-string types — check what `isinstance` guards are already in place

---

### Testing Your Changes

No network access is required. Write a short local test:

```python
from scripts.update_jobs import format_posted_date, get_iso_date

# Should not raise, should return "Unknown" with a warning print
print(format_posted_date("not-a-date"))
print(format_posted_date(None))
print(get_iso_date("garbage input"))
```

Run `python -m py_compile scripts/update_jobs.py` to confirm no syntax errors.
